content:
# 1 Timothy Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TI_003_page_001.jpg

1. The saying is trustworthy: If anyone aspires to the office of overseer, he desires a noble task.

2. Therefore an overseer must be above reproach, the husband of one wife, sober-minded, self-controlled, respectable, hospitable, able to teach,

3. not a drunkard, not violent but gentle, not quarrelsome, not a lover of money.

4. He must manage his own household well, with all dignity keeping his children submissive,

5. for if someone does not know how to manage his own household, how will he care for God's church?

6. He must not be a recent convert, or he may become puffed up with conceit and fall into the condemnation of the devil.

7. Moreover, he must be well thought of by outsiders, so that he may not fall into disgrace, into a snare of the devil.

8. Deacons likewise must be dignified, not double-tongued, not addicted to much wine, not greedy for dishonest gain.

9. They must hold the mystery of the faith with a clear conscience.

10. And let them also be tested first; then let them serve as deacons if they prove themselves blameless.

11. Their wives likewise must be dignified, not slanderers, but sober-minded, faithful in all things.

12. Let deacons each be the husband of one wife, managing their children and their own households well.

13. For those who serve well as deacons gain a good standing for themselves and also great confidence in the faith that is in Christ Jesus.

14. I hope to come to you soon, but I am writing these things to you so that,

15. if I delay, you may know how one ought to behave in the household of God, which is the church of the living God, a pillar and buttress of the truth.

16. Great indeed, we confess, is the mystery of godliness: He was manifested in the flesh, vindicated by the Spirit, seen by angels, proclaimed among the nations, believed on in the world, taken up in glory.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 3 gives qualifications for overseers (vv.1-7) and deacons (vv.8-13), then states the purpose of the letter (vv.14-15) and closes with the Christ Hymn (v.16). The textual variant in v.16 (hos vs. theos) is one of the most significant in the NT.

**Verse 16 — The Christ Hymn:** All three translators followed the Sinaiticus reading (hos/"He who"), not the later theos/"God." SOPHIA affirms: this is textually correct — the original reading is the relative pronoun, later changed to "God" by scribes.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "the oversight" vs. "the office of overseer" vs. "a church leader"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If anyone aspires to [the] oversight" | episkopēs oregetai |
| LOGOS | "If anyone aspires to the office of overseer" | "Office of overseer" — formal |
| PNEUMA | "If someone aspires to be a church leader" | "Church leader" — generic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. episkopē means "oversight/overseership" — a specific role. PNEUMA generalizes to "church leader," losing the specific term that connects to the early church office structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses specific term.

---

### v.2 — "overseer" vs. "church leader" — episkopos

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the overseer" | ton episkopon |
| LOGOS | "an overseer" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a church leader" | Generic — drops episkopos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. episkopos ("overseer") is a specific early church title. PNEUMA's "church leader" loses the technical term. While episkopos later became "bishop," at this stage it denotes a local overseer — LOGOS preserves the term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses technical term.

---

### v.9 — "the mystery of the faith" vs. "the mystery of the faith now revealed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "holding the mystery of the faith in a clean conscience" | to mystērion tēs pisteōs en kathara syneidēsei |
| LOGOS | "They must hold the mystery of the faith with a clear conscience" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "They must be committed to the mystery of the faith now revealed and must live with a clear conscience" | Adds "now revealed"; "committed to" for "holding"; splits into two clauses |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "now revealed" (not in this verse — the "revealed" language appears in 3:16 but not here) and changes "holding" to "committed to" — a different metaphor. The single requirement is split into two.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "now revealed" and splits.

---

### v.11 — "Women" vs. "Their wives" — gynaikas

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Women likewise [must be] dignified" | gynaikas hōsautōs semnas |
| LOGOS | "Their wives likewise must be dignified" | "Their wives" — possessive |
| PNEUMA | "their wives must be respected" | "Their wives" — same |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. gynaikas is ambiguous — "women" or "wives." EMET renders neutrally ("women"), which could refer to women deacons. LOGOS and PNEUMA render as "wives" of the deacons. The parallel structure with hōsautōs ("likewise") — the same word introducing deacons in v.8 — favors "women" as a separate category. However, LOGOS is adopted for consistency, with the note that "women [deacons]" is equally defensible.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. gynaikas hōsautōs may introduce women deacons (a separate category paralleling overseers and deacons) rather than deacons' wives. The "likewise" (hōsautōs) parallels v.8's introduction of deacons. This affects whether the early church recognized women in the diaconate.

---

### v.15 — "pillar and support/buttress" vs. "pillar and foundation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] pillar and support of the truth" | stylos kai hedraiōma tēs alētheias |
| LOGOS | "a pillar and buttress of the truth" | "Buttress" for hedraiōma |
| PNEUMA | "the pillar and foundation of the truth" | "Foundation" for hedraiōma |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. hedraiōma means "support/stay/bulwark" — something that stabilizes from the side, not from beneath. "Buttress" captures this better than "foundation" (which implies something underneath). The church upholds truth, not undergirds it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — "buttress" vs. "foundation."

---

### v.16 — "He who was revealed in the flesh" vs. "Christ was revealed in a human body"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He who was revealed in [the] flesh" | hos ephanerōthē en sarki |
| LOGOS | "He was manifested in the flesh" | Same — "manifested" for ephanerōthē |
| PNEUMA | "Christ was revealed in a human body" | Names "Christ"; "human body" for sarx |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Sinaiticus reads hos ("He who") — the subject is the "mystery of godliness" from the preceding clause. PNEUMA names "Christ" (not in this clause) and changes "flesh" (sarx) to "human body" — a different concept. sarx in this hymnic context means the entire sphere of fleshly, mortal existence, not just a physical body.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA names Christ and narrows sarx to "human body."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA loses technical terms | 2 | episkopos → "church leader" (vv.1-2) |
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 1 | "Now revealed" (v.9) |
| PNEUMA narrows terms | 1 | sarx → "human body" (v.16) |
| gynaikas ambiguity | 1 | "Women [deacons]" vs. "wives [of deacons]" (v.11) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 3's most significant disagreement is the gynaikas ambiguity (v.11) — whether Paul introduces women deacons or deacons' wives. The parallel structure with hōsautōs supports women deacons, but the textual evidence is genuinely ambiguous. The Christ Hymn (v.16) shows PNEUMA naming "Christ" where the Greek has only a pronoun, and narrowing sarx ("flesh") to "human body."

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
